metadata.title: States PCS: Jharkhand GK for JPSC and other Jharkhand State Exams

questions.question: Jain idols from which century were found in Benusagar, Singhbhum district?
questions.explanation: Jain idols recovered from Benusagar in Singhbhum district date to the 7th century. J.D. Beglar in 1875 AD dated the site to this century based on sculptures. Archaeological findings revealed habitation from the 5th century AD onward, but Jain idols specifically are attributed to the 7th century. Singhbhum district is located in the Indian state of Jharkhand.

questions.question: Who was the Nag dynasty ruler during the Mughal invasion?
questions.explanation: According to Abul Fazl’s records, Madhukar Shah was the zamindar ruler of Kokrah during the first Mughal invasion around 1585 AD. Emperor Akbar’s general Shahbaz Khan Kamboh led the invasion and subdued the Nagvanshi kingdom in Jharkhand’s Chotanagpur region. Madhukar Shah refused to pay tribute initially but was later forced to become a tributary, marking the start of Mughal influence over the Nag dynasty.

questions.question: Followers of Birsa Munda saw him as incarnation of which God?
questions.explanation: Birsa Munda led the Ulgulan rebellion in Jharkhand’s Chotanagpur region in the late 19th century. Followers recognized him as an incarnation of God (Singbonga), prophet, and Father of the Earth (Dharti Aaba). He founded the Birsait faith, rejected Christianity, promoted worship of one God, and sought to end British rule by proclaiming Munda Raj. Tribal groups like Mundas, Oraons, and Kharias revered him as a divine figure opposing colonial land alienation.

questions.question: Who shifted Chota Nagpur Nag dynasty’s capital to Palkot from Doisa?
questions.explanation: Yadunath Shah shifted the Nag dynasty capital from Doisa (Navratangarh) to Palkot between 1717 and 1719 AD. This relocation happened after Sarbuland Khan’s invasion during Emperor Muhammad Shah’s reign. Yadunath Shah agreed to pay one lakh rupees as Nazrana to the Mughals. Palkot was chosen for its stronger defensive advantages. Remnants such as the Lalgarh fort and temples remain from the Nagvanshi period in Palkot.

questions.question: Who was an associate of Thakur Vishwanath Shahdev in the 1857 rebellion?
questions.explanation: Ganpat Rai, also known as Pandey Ganpat Rai, was a key associate of Thakur Vishwanath Shahdev during the 1857 Indian Rebellion. Thakur Vishwanath Shahdev was the king of Barkagarh estate and led a major uprising against British rule. His associates included several zamindars and local leaders such as Ganpat Rai, Tikait Umrao Singh, and Sheikh Bhikhari. Ganpat Rai played a significant role in organizing the Mukti Vahini (people’s army) alongside Thakur Vishwanath Shahdev in this rebellion.

questions.question: Who led the Santhals of Borogera village in the Civil Disobedience Movement?
questions.explanation: Bangam Mane, also called Bangam Manjhi, from Borogera village, Gomiya thana, Hazaribagh, led the Santhals during the Civil Disobedience Movement. He was recognized as a spiritual leader who claimed supernatural abilities. Santhals under his leadership took part in protests against British rule. This movement was separate from the 1855 Santhal Rebellion, led by Sidhu and Kanhu Murmu in other regions. The Santhal protests in Hazaribagh were aligned with Gandhi’s civil disobedience campaigns and are documented in historical records.

questions.question: When was Thakur Vishwanath Singh hanged?
questions.explanation: Thakur Vishwanath Singh was hanged on April 16, 1858, during the 1857 Revolt. He led the rebellion in Chotanagpur, Jharkhand. After the Battle of Chatra in October 1857, he initially escaped but was later captured. His execution took place by hanging from a kadam tree branch. This date is confirmed by reports and artifacts displayed in the Hotwar museum in Ranchi. The hanging happened after his capture post-rebellion activities against the British forces.

questions.question: In which year was the Ramgarh session of the Indian National Congress held?
questions.explanation: The Ramgarh session was the 53rd All India Congress Committee meeting held from March 18-20, 1940. It took place near Ranchi, Jharkhand, and was presided over by Maulana Abul Kalam Azad. Key leaders like Mahatma Gandhi and Jawaharlal Nehru attended. The session protested India’s involvement in World War II without consent and demanded complete independence. It contributed to the foundation for the Quit India Movement launched in 1942. The venue is now part of the Punjab Regimental Centre.

questions.question: What was the name of the hand-written magazine in Hazaribagh jail during Civil Disobedience?
questions.explanation: Ramvriksha Benipuri, Mahamaya Prasad Sinha, and Bhavani Dayal Sanyasi published the hand-written magazine “Karagar” while imprisoned in Hazaribagh jail during the Civil Disobedience Movement. Hazaribagh jail held many Congress activists from the Bihar-Jharkhand region after the Second Round Table Conference in 1931. The magazine was part of underground efforts to maintain the nationalist spirit during British repression. Jayaprakash Narayan was also detained in the same jail before his famous escape.

questions.question: What is another name for the Birsa Munda revolution?
questions.explanation: Birsa Munda’s revolution took place in 1899 in the Chhotanagpur region, now in Jharkhand. It is called the Ulgulan revolution, meaning “great tumult” or “revolution” in the Mundari language. The movement aimed to end British rule, reject landlord rents, and establish Munda Raj under the slogan “Abua Disom, Abua Raj” (our country, our rule). The Ulgulan gathered around 7000 tribals and was distinct from the Ho, Hul, and Chero revolts in India.
